text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for the dissection during the robotic esophagectomy, occasionally switching to scissors or other instruments as needed.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the lesser omentum, using surgical instruments to carefully separate this tissue from the surrounding structures.
The caudate lobe of the liver is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the omental tissue, revealing the right crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon clears the area around the right cruciate of the diaphragm, preparing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon places a holding suture in the surgical field, securing tissue in preparation for further steps in the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon places a traction suture over the xiphoid process, securing it to facilitate further surgical manipulation.
A straight long needle is used to pull a suture, likely over the xiphoid process area, as part of the traction suture placement.
The surgeon sutures the pulling suture to the crux of the diaphragm, securing it in place.
The surgeon places a suture that retracts the liver upwards, providing better access to the surgical field.
